div.p: Problem: Two memory accesses per call Every function call requires:

ul.li: Branch only when counter hits zero The branch is highly predictable!

g.text: L1 Data Cache | 32 KB | ~4 cycles | 90%+ hit rate
L2 Cache | 256 KB | ~12 cycles | 95%+ hit rate
L3 Cache | 8 MB | ~40 cycles | Shared across cores
Main Memory | GBs | ~150 cycles | Throughput limited
Counter in L1 | After warmup | ~4 cycle access
Key Insight: The branch is 'free' because it's highly predictable. The CPU executes it in parallel with other work.

ul.li: Signed comparison handles edge cases correctly

g.text: mov eax, [rdi + func_id*4] ; LOAD: ~100 cycles if miss
mov [rdi + func_id*4], eax ; STORE: ~100 cycles if miss

text.tspan: // Counter hit zero — this function is hot
// Counter hit zero — this function is hot

g.text: Total overhead: 999 calls × 1 cycle + 1 mispredict × 15 cycles

text.tspan: Total overhead: 999 calls × 1 cycle + 1 mispredict × 15 cycles

div.p: How branch prediction works: Modern CPUs use sophisticated predictors:

ul.li: Two-level adaptive: Tracks history of each branch
Tournament predictor: Chooses best of multiple algorithms
Perceptron-based: Neural network prediction (modern Intel) For our decrement-and-branch:

g.text: Call #1: Counter in RAM → L3 → L2 → L1 (150 cycles)
Calls #2-1000: Counter in L1 (4 cycles each)
Call #1000: Trigger JIT, counter never accessed again

text.tspan: Call #1: Counter in RAM → L3 → L2 → L1 (150 cycles)
Calls #2-1000: Counter in L1 (4 cycles each)
Call #1000: Trigger JIT, counter never accessed again

g.text: int32_t invocation_counter; // Signed for overflow detect

// Returns true only when counter hits zero
